description:
A script developer has left you a tiny “legacy utility” to test.  
    1. Inside the directory “/home/user/legacy_utils” there is an old-school Bourne-again shell script named “greet.sh”.  
    2. Your goal is to run the script once, passing the single argument “LinuxLearner”, and capture everything it prints into a new log file.  

    Required end-state after you are finished:
      • A *new* directory “/home/user/logs” must exist.  
      • A *new* file “/home/user/logs/greet_run.log” must exist.  
      • The log file must contain **exactly two lines** and nothing else:  
            Line-1:  greet.sh version 1.0  
            Line-2:  Hello, LinuxLearner!  
        (No extra whitespace before or after the lines; include the exclamation mark; use a single newline character after each line.)  

    Hints and constraints:
      • You do not need root or sudo.  
      • If the script is not yet executable, make it so.  
      • You may use any combination of standard command-line tools (e.g. “chmod”, “mkdir”, shell redirection or “tee”) but keep it to a simple sequence of 2-3 commands.  
      • The automated grader will strictly compare the content of “/home/user/logs/greet_run.log” against the two-line format shown above, so be precise.  
      • Produce no other files in “/home/user/logs”.
truth:
# Pre-existing file placed in the image before the agent starts
    /home/user/legacy_utils/greet.sh  (permissions 644)
    -------------------------------
    #!/bin/bash
    echo "greet.sh version 1.0"
    echo "Hello, $1!"
    -------------------------------

    # After correct task completion the grader will look for:
    Target-file: /home/user/logs/greet_run.log
    Exact-content (two lines, LF-terminated):
    greet.sh version 1.0
    Hello, LinuxLearner!

    # No trailing blank lines or spaces are allowed.
name: task_000000_7ee5df44
